audit_trail.notes: Three reviewers completed two full adversarial cycles. Gemini maintained a low score (4/10) across both cycles with high confidence (0.95), citing the main text/appendix contradiction as fatal and the topological equivalence argument as formally unsupported. Claude and codex converged downward from cycle 1 to cycle 2 (claude: 6→5.5, codex: 6.4→5.8) after accepting gemini's physical reasoning that the appendix waveform description is more coherent and claude's observation that shared q̃ does not uniquely determine the (z,w,v) triple. The most contested issue is severity: gemini treats the branch-label contradiction as fatal; claude and codex treat it as a major but correctable presentation error. Mechanical average final score: 5.1/10. Fiducial parameter values were unrecoverable from the HTML source throughout both cycles.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper argues that certain exotic black holes (with both electric and magnetic charges) can trap orbiting objects in multiple distinct "pockets" of gravity, unlike ordinary black holes which have only one. Two orbits that look topologically the same by one standard classification can still radiate gravitational waves quite differently because they sit in different pockets. The review board found the central idea interesting but identified a likely labeling error in the waveform descriptions and an incomplete proof that the orbits are truly in the same topological class — both issues require correction before the main claim is fully substantiated.
human_spotchecks: Verify in the original PDF figures whether the inner (red, deepest-well) branch in Fig. 4 displays burst-like or smooth/sinusoidal waveforms, and confirm that the color/label mapping is consistent between the main text (Figs. 4–5) and the appendix (Figs. 6–7). This directly determines whether the main text or the appendix description is correct.

Determine whether the three periodic orbit branches in Fig. 3 share identical (z, w, v) Levin-Perez-Giz triples or only the same rational rotation number q̃. This requires tracing each orbit numerically and counting zooms, whirls, and vertices; the paper does not display these values and the text never asserts z or v are equal.

Retrieve the specific fiducial physical parameter values (total mass M in solar masses, luminosity distance in Mpc or Gpc) used to produce Fig. 5. These appear to have been dropped in the LaTeX-to-HTML conversion and are necessary to verify that spectral peaks fall in the correct frequency bands for LISA/Taiji/TianQin.

Confirm that all three potential wells and their associated bound-orbit domains lie in the regular exterior region of the dyonic black hole (outside all horizons) for the stated parameter values, ruling out any well located inside a horizon or near a pathological region.
public_summary: The paper studies extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) — systems where a small compact object spirals into a much heavier black hole — around a class of exotic black holes that carry both electric and magnetic charge and are governed by a nonlinear electromagnetic theory. Unlike ordinary Schwarzschild black holes, whose gravitational potential has only one "well" that can trap orbiting matter, the paper argues these dyonic black holes can have multiple nested wells, each capable of hosting its own family of bound orbits. The central result the paper claims is that two periodic orbits which carry identical topological labels (under the Levin-Perez-Giz classification scheme) can nevertheless radiate gravitational waves with very different waveform shapes, because they occupy different wells and therefore experience different spacetime curvature. Gravitational waveforms are computed using a semi-relativistic kludge approximation and overlaid against projected sensitivity curves for LISA, Taiji, TianQin, DECIGO, and BBO. The reviewers found the underlying physics plausible and the multi-well orbit picture numerically demonstrated for one parameter choice. However, two issues were flagged across all review cycles: a direct contradiction between the main text and the appendix regarding which branch produces burst-like versus smooth signals (a six-page paper cannot sustain such an inconsistency), and an incomplete argument for topological equivalence — the paper claims equivalence from shared q̃ and winding number w, but the Levin-Perez-Giz taxonomy requires the full triple (z, w, v) to be identical, and the paper never verifies the zoom count z or vertex count v match across branches. The claim that signals would be detectable is supported only by sensitivity-curve overlap, without signal-to-noise ratio calculations.
reviewer_disagreements: Severity of the main text/appendix contradiction: gemini classified it as 'fatal' and grounds for rejection at the current form; claude and codex classified it as 'major' but correctable — a likely labeling or description error that does not invalidate the underlying numerical results. The board did not resolve this dispute; it is a severity judgment, not a factual one.

Overall significance of the result: gemini argues that two orbits with different energies and periods trivially produce different gravitational waves, making the 'radiatively distinct' framing conceptually vacuous; claude and codex counter that the non-trivial claim is whether a shared topological label predicts radiative character — which it does not in a multi-well geometry — and that this diagnostic point has genuine value even if the raw radiative difference is unsurprising.

Recommended verdict: gemini recommended 'weak_evidence' (score 4/10) in both cycles; claude recommended 'promising' (score 5.5/10); codex moved from 'promising' to 'contested' (score 5.8/10) after cycle 2 cross-review. The mechanical average settled at 5.1/10.

strongest_objections.objection: The main text and appendix give directly inverted descriptions of which orbital branch is burst-like versus smooth. The main text states the inner branch generates burst-like signals with sharp periapsis spikes while the extended branch shows the weakest modulation; the appendix states the innermost (E_1) branch has smooth, nearly circular motion and a narrow-band sinusoidal signal, while the highest-energy cross-well branch (E_3) produces broadband burst-like emission. Physical reasoning (a tightly confined deep-well orbit is more circular; the cross-well branch has the most complex dynamics) strongly supports the appendix account, making the main text description likely incorrect or mislabeled. This is a critical presentation error in a six-page letter whose central figures purport to display the effect.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Main text discussion of Fig. 4 vs. Appendix discussion of Figs. 6–7

strongest_objections.objection: The topological equivalence claim rests solely on shared rational rotation number q̃ and winding number w, but the Levin-Perez-Giz classification requires the full triple (z, w, v) — zoom count, whirl count, and vertex count — to be identical. Multiple distinct (z, w, v) triples can produce the same q̃. The paper never displays or verifies that z and v match across branches. Compounding this, Fig. 3 explicitly describes 'distinct zoom-whirl morphologies' for the three supposedly topologically equivalent orbits, which is precisely what different topological classes look like. If z or v differs, the headline claim of 'topologically equivalent yet radiatively distinct' is formally incorrect.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Eqs. (8)–(9), Fig. 3 caption, 'Multi-branch periodic orbits' section

strongest_objections.objection: The kludge quadrupole approximation is applied to the inner branch, which is described as dominated by rapid whirl motion near the black hole horizon — precisely the deep strong-field regime where the authors themselves acknowledge the approximation 'is not strictly valid.' Quantitative spectral properties and amplitude hierarchy for this branch could differ substantially under a Teukolsky-based or self-force calculation. The claim that radiative differences 'are expected to persist beyond any specific waveform prescription' is asserted rather than demonstrated.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 'Gravitational-wave emissions', paragraph introducing kludge waveform and Eq. (10)

strongest_objections.objection: The paper repeatedly uses 'generically' to characterize multi-well behavior and multi-branch periodic orbits, yet demonstrates the effect only for a single numerical parameter set with no analytic condition on (q_e, q_m, α) that guarantees multiple wells, and no parameter-space scan or phase diagram. The astrophysical relevance of the signatures depends critically on whether multi-well geometry requires fine-tuning.

Detector accessibility to LISA, Taiji, TianQin, DECIGO, and BBO is argued on the basis of overlaying characteristic strain spectra against sensitivity curves, without providing signal-to-noise ratio estimates, observation duration, sky-averaging, detector response, or confusion noise treatment. Even an order-of-magnitude SNR estimate is standard for a Letter claiming observational accessibility.

strongest_supported_claims: For the specific dyonic black hole parameter set displayed, the effective radial potential for massive test particles develops three nested wells, in contrast to the single well of the Schwarzschild spacetime. This follows directly from the analytic metric function and is illustrated in Fig. 1.

At fixed angular momentum, three coexisting branches of bound periodic orbits exist within the multi-well dyonic potential, each localized in a different well or spanning multiple wells. Their existence is demonstrated numerically via the q̃-vs-energy plot (Fig. 2).

Under the kludge quadrupole approximation, the three orbital branches produce qualitatively distinct gravitational waveform morphologies and characteristic strain spectra, reflecting the different curvature environments of each branch (Figs. 4–5). The qualitative distinctness is robust within this approximation.

The coexisting periodic branches differ in amplitude hierarchy, phase structure, and spectral weight distribution, with the extended cross-well branch exhibiting multi-lobed velocity patterns and sharp acceleration bursts at turning points, as shown in the appendix phase-space analysis (Fig. 6).
technical_takeaway: The paper aims to show that periodic orbits in a dyonic black hole spacetime (quasi-topological nonlinear electrodynamics) can share the same rational rotation number q̃ and winding number w — and thus nominally the same Levin-Perez-Giz topological class — yet produce gravitational waveforms with distinct amplitude modulations and harmonic spectra under the kludge quadrupole approximation. The review board identified two critical issues that survived adversarial cross-review: (1) the Levin-Perez-Giz class is fully specified by the triple (z, w, v), not q̃ alone, and the paper never verifies that z and v match across branches — Fig. 3 even notes "distinct zoom-whirl morphologies," which is inconsistent with the claimed topological equivalence; (2) the main text and appendix give inverted descriptions of which branch is burst-like versus smooth, with physical reasoning favoring the appendix account (a tightly confined deep-well orbit is more circular, not burst-like). The waveform calculations rely on a kludge approximation that the authors themselves acknowledge is invalid in the deep strong-field regime used for the inner branch. Detector accessibility is claimed from sensitivity-curve overlap without any SNR estimate.
